rulings.Case Title: NEW LIVABLE CALIFORNIA ET AL VS. ASSOCIATION OF BAY AREA GOVERNMENTS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Award Of Reasonable Attorney Fees
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONERS NEW LIVABLE CALIFORNIA, COMMUNITY VENTURE PARTNERS, INC'S. Motion For Award Of Reasonable Attorney Fees (PART 1 OF 2) Petitioners/Plaintiffs' motion for award of reasonable attorney fees is denied. On September 10, 2019, after sustaining without leave to amend Defendants' demurrer to the petition for writ of mandate and complaint for injunctive and declaratory relief, the Court entered judgment against Petitioners, and directed that they "shall take nothing by reason of their complaint" and that the action be dismissed with prejudice. Petitioners are not entitled to attorneys' fees under the Ralph M. Brown Act. Government Code section 54960.5 authorizes a court to award reasonable attorney fees to the plaintiff only "where it is found that a legislative body of the local agency has violated this chapter" or an action is dismissed because a legislative body has provided an unconditional commitment pursuant to section 54960.2(c)(1). The Court made no finding that Respondent violated the Brown Act. Further, Petitioners concede that Respondent did not provide an unconditional commitment to cease and desist from past alleged violations within the meaning of the statute, and the verbal statement by Respondent's Vice President did not meet any of the statutory requirements for such a commitment. (See Gov. Code § 54960.2(c)(1), (2) [commitment must be made in writing within 30 days of receiving cease and desist letter, must state that commitment may be rescinded only by majority vote, and shall be approved by the legislative body in open session as a separate item of business].) Petitioners' alternative request for attorneys' fees under section 1021.5 of the Code of Civil Procedure is ill-founded. Petitioners were not the "successful party," as they did not prevail on any issue in the litigation, judgment was entered against them, and they have appealed. (See Riverwatch v. County of San Diego Dept. of Environmental Health (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 768, 783 [the successful party under section 1021.5 is generally the "prevailing party," that is, the party that "succeeds on any significant issue in litigation which achieves some of the benefit the parties sought in bringing suit."].) As for Petitioners' catalyst theory, the Brown Act prescribes the conditions under which a court may award attorneys' fees to a party where the legislative body makes an unconditional commitment to cease and desist from an alleged violation of the Act. As the more specific statute, it controls. (See State Dept. of Public Health v. Superior Court (2015) 60 Cal.4th 940, 960-961.) In any event, Petitioners have not shown that their lawsuit-which was filed two weeks after the verbal statement that they now claim constituted a commitment-was a catalyst motivating Respondent to provide the primary relief sought in their complaint. (See Coalition for a Sustainable Future in Yucaipa v. City of Yucaipa (2015) 238 Cal.App.4th 513, 521 ["At the very least, a plaintiff must establish the precise factual/legal condition that it sought to change or affect as a prerequisite for establishing the catalytic effect of its lawsuit."].) Petitioners did not achieve the primary relief they sought, which was to set aside Respondent's actions approving the CASA Compact. (END OF TENTATIVE RULING PART 1 OF 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: REDWOOD ELECTRIC GROUP, INC., VS. THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2019, Line 2, REAL PARTY IN INTEREST DPR CONSTRUCTION, A GENERAL PARTNERSHIP'S DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION Real Party in Interest DPR Construction's demurrer to the third cause of action for declaratory and injunctive relief is sustained without leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 1061 ["The court may refuse to exercise the power granted by this chapter in any case where its declaration or determination is not necessary or proper at the time under all the circumstances."] The gravamen of the third cause of action contests respondent Regents' conduct during the course of the substitution administrative hearing. (Complaint, 90-95.) "It is settled that an action for declaratory relief is not appropriate to review an administrative decision." (State of California v. Superior Court (1974) 12 Cal.3d 237, 249; Tejon Real Estate, LLC v. City of Los Angeles (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 149, 154-155 [declaratory relief proper only to declare statute unconstitutional on face, and not as applied to plaintiff by an administrative agency].) Declaratory relief also cannot be joined with a writ of mandate reviewing an administrative determination. (City of Pasadena v. Cohen (2014) 228 Cal.App.4th 1461, 1467; Guilbert v. Regents of University of California (1979) 93 Cal.App.3d 233, 244.) Plaintiff's declaratory relief claim runs afoul of this authority and it fails to show how the claim is necessary to accord complete relief. Plaintiff's reliance on Venice Town Council, Inc. v. City of Los Angeles (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 1547 is misplaced. There, the court concluded that the declaratory relief claim was well-pleaded because the appellants did "not challenge any particular decision or order…Instead, appellants seek to resolve the City's fundamental misunderstanding of its responsibilities under the Mello Act to avoid continued violations or nonenforcement in the future. Declaratory relief has been held to be the proper remedy when it is alleged an agency has a policy of ignoring or violating applicable laws." (Id. at 1566.) In addition, it appears that the issues that Plaintiff raises in its opposition regarding DPR's alleged violations of the Fair Practices Act can be resolved in the parties' companion litigation. (See CGC-19-577544.) In fact, that action presently includes allegations regarding DPR's alleged violations of the Fair Practices Act. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for DPR is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BRENDA MOORE ET AL VS. MEGAN ROHRER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2019, Line 4, CROSS DEFENDANTS BRENDA MOORE, ANDREA MURPHY, TRAVIS O'HARA, BERNICE LASSITER, ADAH BUIKE, JEANNIE WILSON, JAMES ROWLEY, PAMELA RYAN, SALLY RYAN 'S DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT Off calendar. Counsel failed to comply with Code of Civ. Proc., § 430.41. That statute requires that counsel meet and confer "in person or by telephone" to determine "whether an agreement can be reached that would resolve the objections to be raised in the demurrer." Plaintiffs' counsel admits in his declaration that he did not comply with the statute before filing the demurrer. The parties are ordered to meet and confer. Plaintiff has until January 17, 2020 to respond to the cross-complaint. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: HANFORD FREUND & COMPANY ET AL VS. HOMELAND INSURANCE COMPANY OF NEW YORK, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT HOMELAND INSURANCE COMPANY OF NEW YORK'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication Off calendar. Homeland filed its motion for summary judgment on June 12, 2019 and the motion was directed to plaintiff's first amended complaint. On October 23, 2019, plaintiff filed a second amended complaint. The motion is therefore moot. (See State Comp. Ins. Fund v. Superior Court (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 1124, 1131 ["Because there is but one complaint in a civil action [citation], the filing of an amended complaint moots a motion directed to a prior complaint."].) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MARIA ALFARO VS. RYAN JUSTIN CHAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses And To Strike Improper Objections To Request For Admission
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFFs MARIA ALFARO, LUIS ALFARO, LUIS ALFARO, and JASLENE ALFARO's Motion To Compel Further Responses And To Strike Improper Objections To Request For Admission. Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiffs Maria and Luis Alfaro's (collectively the "Alfaros'") Motion to Compel Further Responses and to Strike Improper Objections to Requests for Admission is GRANTED AS UNOPPOSED. The Alfaros' request for monetary sanctions against Defendant Ryan Chan (not counsel) is GRANTED AS UNOPPOSED. All other sanctions, if any, are DENIED. The Alfaros shall prepare a form of the order recapitulating this tentative ruling, shall state in the proposed order the exact remedy sought as against each particular Response to Request for Admission, and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NOEL CLAY VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion & Motion To Quash Subpoenas And For Attorney?S Fees And Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF NOEL CLAY'S Motion To Quash Subpoenas And For Attorney’S Fees And Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Noel Clay ("Clay") brings a Motion to quash Subpoenas. Defendant, the City and County of San Francisco (the "City") defends the motion arguing it is moot and sanctions are unwarranted in light of the Parties's agreement to withdraw and/or modify said subpoenas. The Court refrains from issuing a tentative ruling on the Motion's ultimate issue at this time. [FULL TENTATIVE SENT VIA EMAIL TO COUNSEL BY JPT] The Court finds that the subpoenas seeking information as to "all" of Clay's medical conditions, are overbroad. Accordingly, Parties are ordered to appear and be prepared to discuss (1) the facts of the meet and confer, and specifically the timing of the Instant Motion, (2) whether the remedy sought is moot or not, and (3) whether sanctions are warranted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
